prompt: Find the verse work in the corpus whose opening describes a Roman emperor encamped on the bank of the Danube against a hostile alliance specifically named as 'the Hunnish people and Hrethgoths,' 'Franks and Hugas,' and which then narrates the same emperor's pre-battle dream of a heavenly herald revealing a gem-adorned cross inscribed with letters of light bearing the message 'By this sign, conquer.' Quote the named-tribes line and the cross-inscription verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The four-tribe muster (Huns, Hrethgoths, Franks, Hugas) on the Danube paired with the angelic 'By this sign, conquer' Cross-vision is uniquely the opening of Cynewulf's Elene; Andreas opens in Mermedonia with cannibals, Juliana in Nicomedia under Maximian, and the Old English elegies are not narrative imperial verse — none stage a Constantinian Danube muster.

prompt: Find the verse work in the corpus in which a queen, after starving a reluctant Hebrew informant for seven nights in a dry well, is led to a hill where the man digs 'twenty feet deep' and unearths 'three crosses together' from a dark cavern; the queen then has all three set up in the city until a dead youth is brought on a bier at the ninth hour and revives only when the third cross is raised over his body. Quote the depth-and-three-crosses passage and the corpse-revival passage verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The seven-nights-in-a-dry-well ordeal, the precise 'twenty feet deep' excavation yielding three crosses, and the ninth-hour identification-by-resurrection test are the signature narrative beats of the Inventio Crucis legend as rendered in Cynewulf's Elene; no other corpus work — including Andreas (which has tortures but no cross-discovery) and Juliana (a virgin-martyr passion) — contains this triple-cross discrimination test.

prompt: Find the verse work in the corpus whose closing passage laments an old man 'tossed by waves of care' who once 'in the mead-hall received treasures, appled gold' and rode where 'the horse measured the mile-paths before him, proudly prancing, adorned with wires,' and which then transitions into an eschatological fire-judgment that sorts every soul into exactly three ranks within the flame — the righteous 'uppermost in that fire,' the sinful tormented 'in the middle, covered with soot,' and the accursed cast 'in the fire's depths' down into hell's abyss. Quote the 'appled gold' mead-hall line and the three-tier fire-judgment passage verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The personalised mead-hall lament of an aged poet ('appled gold,' the horse measuring mile-paths 'adorned with wires') flowing directly into a tripartite fire-purgatory that divides humanity into uppermost / middle / lowest within a single flame is the closing signature of Cynewulf's Elene; the three-rank fire sorting is absent from Andreas, from the differently-framed Juliana epilogue, and from the Old English elegies (which lament transience but do not stage a tripartite judgment-fire).
